content.text: Incident Classification Decision Tree

The first minutes of an incident are the most consequential for time-to-mitigate. The classification decision tree gives incident responders a deterministic path from initial alert or user report to a (category, severity) pair in under two minutes, so the right on-call team is paged immediately, the right runbook is opened, and the right escalation path is activated—without requiring domain expertise from the first responder.
The Decision Tree

The tree has two root branches: the first question distinguishes proactive detection (alerts fired before users noticed) from reactive detection (user reports arrived first). This single question determines whether safety and security questions need answering before severity is assigned.

attrs.alt: Incident classification decision tree for agentic AI systems

content.text: Branch 1: Proactive Detection (No User Impact Yet)

When an alert fires before any user reports arrive, the first responder asks whether infrastructure or tool-call error codes are elevated. If yes, the incident is Infrastructure. If no, the incident is Capability—the service is returning technically valid responses that are semantically degraded.
Infrastructure signals: HTTP 429, 500, or 503 from upstream LLM endpoint; GPU OOM restart events; p99 latency spike; container crash-loop.
Capability signals: output-quality eval score below threshold; agent step-count exceeding max_steps; token-usage ratio near 1.0; repeated action hash.
Default severity for proactive detection: SEV-3 until user impact is confirmed, then re-evaluate.
Branch 2: Reactive Detection (Users Reporting Impact)

When a user report triggers the incident, the first responder immediately asks whether harmful output or security indicators are present. This question must be answered first because Safety and Security incidents require notifications beyond the engineering on-call rotation—Legal, Trust and Safety, CISO—and those notifications must happen within minutes, not after a 30-minute triage.
Check the user's report for mentions of harmful, offensive, or dangerous content. If present: Safety path.
Check recent tool-call traces for anomalous sequences: unexpected data reads, POST calls to external endpoints not in the tool manifest, high-volume credential lookups. If present: Security path.
If neither is present, determine whether the failure is operational (wrong outcome from a correct tool call) or technical (error returned to user). Route to Integration or Capability respectively.
Decision Tree as Code

Encode the decision tree in Python so it can be embedded in your alerting webhook handler, PagerDuty enrichment Lambda, or incident CLI tool. The function takes a signals dict and returns a Classification namedtuple.

"""classification_tree.py -- decision tree for AI incident triage."""
from __future__ import annotations
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional


@dataclass
class Classification:
    category: str       # capability | infrastructure | safety | integration | security
    severity: str       # SEV-1 | SEV-2 | SEV-3 | SEV-4
    owner_team: str
    page_immediately: bool
    notify_legal: bool
    notify_ciso: bool
    rationale: str


def classify(
    user_reported: bool,
    safety_indicators: bool,
    security_indicators: bool,
    infra_error_elevated: bool,
    user_harm_confirmed: bool,
    output_quality_degraded: bool,
    tool_auth_error: bool,
) -> Classification:
    """Traverse the classification decision tree.

    All boolean parameters correspond to signals that should be pre-computed
    from metrics, trace data, and user report text before calling this function.
    """
    if not user_reported:
        # Proactive detection: determine infra vs capability
        if infra_error_elevated:
            sev = "SEV-2" if output_quality_degraded else "SEV-3"
            return Classification(
                category="infrastructure",
                severity=sev,
                owner_team="sre",
                page_immediately=(sev == "SEV-2"),
                notify_legal=False,
                notify_ciso=False,
                rationale="Alert-detected infrastructure error with no confirmed user impact yet.",
            )
        else:
            return Classification(
                category="capability",
                severity="SEV-3",
                owner_team="ai-platform",
                page_immediately=False,
                notify_legal=False,
                notify_ciso=False,
                rationale="Proactive signal: output quality or agent loop degradation detected.",
            )

    # Reactive: user reported impact
    if security_indicators:
        return Classification(
            category="security",
            severity="SEV-1",
            owner_team="security",
            page_immediately=True,
            notify_legal=True,
            notify_ciso=True,
            rationale="Security breach indicators (prompt injection or data exfiltration) present.",
        )

    if safety_indicators:
        sev = "SEV-1" if user_harm_confirmed else "SEV-2"
        return Classification(
            category="safety",
            severity=sev,
            owner_team="trust-and-safety",
            page_immediately=True,
            notify_legal=(sev == "SEV-1"),
            notify_ciso=False,
            rationale="Safety policy violation detected in user-reported interaction.",
        )

    if tool_auth_error or infra_error_elevated:
        return Classification(
            category="integration",
            severity="SEV-2",
            owner_team="platform-integrations",
            page_immediately=True,
            notify_legal=False,
            notify_ciso=False,
            rationale="User-impacting failure traced to tool or integration layer.",
        )

    # Fallback: capability failure with user impact
    return Classification(
        category="capability",
        severity="SEV-2",
        owner_team="ai-platform",
        page_immediately=True,
        notify_legal=False,
        notify_ciso=False,
        rationale="User-reported impact from degraded model output quality.",
    )


if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Example: user reported harmful output, harm confirmed
    result = classify(
        user_reported=True,
        safety_indicators=True,
        security_indicators=False,
        infra_error_elevated=False,
        user_harm_confirmed=True,
        output_quality_degraded=False,
        tool_auth_error=False,
    )
    print(f"Category: {result.category}")
    print(f"Severity: {result.severity}")
    print(f"Owner: {result.owner_team}")
    print(f"Page immediately: {result.page_immediately}")
    print(f"Notify legal: {result.notify_legal}")
    print(f"Rationale: {result.rationale}")
Signal Extraction for Decision Inputs
The decision tree is only as reliable as its input signals. Each boolean input must be computed from concrete, observable sources.
Decision Input
Primary Signal Source
Threshold / Condition
user_reported
Support ticket, in-app feedback, social media monitor
Any inbound report in the last 15 min
safety_indicators
Safety classifier block log, user report text keywords
Any block in session or harmful-content keyword match
security_indicators
Anomalous tool-call sequence detector, injection scanner
Any injection flag or unauthorized data access in trace
infra_error_elevated
LLM HTTP status codes, container restart counter
http_4xx_5xx_rate > 0.05 OR container_restarts > 0
user_harm_confirmed
Support agent review of session transcript
Human confirmation within 5 min of initial report
output_quality_degraded
Output eval pipeline, LLM-as-judge score stream
5m avg eval score < 0.6
tool_auth_error
Tool-call HTTP status metrics
tool_call_http_401_rate > 0.02
PagerDuty Enrichment Integration

Wire the classify() function into your alert-routing webhook so that every incoming PagerDuty event receives a classification label before routing. The following FastAPI endpoint enriches an incoming alert payload and re-routes it to the correct service.

"""alert_enrichment.py -- PagerDuty enrichment webhook."""
from fastapi import FastAPI, HTTPException
from pydantic import BaseModel
import httpx
from classification_tree import classify
from metrics_client import MetricsClient  # internal abstraction

app = FastAPI()
metrics = MetricsClient(prometheus_url="http://prometheus:9090")

PD_ROUTING = {
    "sre": "PD_SERVICE_ID_SRE",
    "ai-platform": "PD_SERVICE_ID_AI_PLATFORM",
    "trust-and-safety": "PD_SERVICE_ID_TRUST_SAFETY",
    "security": "PD_SERVICE_ID_SECURITY",
    "platform-integrations": "PD_SERVICE_ID_INTEGRATIONS",
}


class AlertPayload(BaseModel):
    alert_key: str
    description: str
    source: str  # "user_report" | "prometheus" | "synthetic"
    session_id: str | None = None


@app.post("/enrich-alert")
async def enrich_alert(payload: AlertPayload):
    user_reported = payload.source == "user_report"

    # Pull live signals for this session or globally
    signals = metrics.query_snapshot(
        session_id=payload.session_id,
        metrics=[
            "safety_classifier_blocks_total",
            "tool_calls_flagged_injection_total",
            "llm_http_5xx_rate",
            "output_quality_eval_score",
            "tool_call_http_401_rate",
        ],
    )

    cls = classify(
        user_reported=user_reported,
        safety_indicators=signals["safety_classifier_blocks_total"] > 0,
        security_indicators=signals["tool_calls_flagged_injection_total"] > 0,
        infra_error_elevated=signals["llm_http_5xx_rate"] > 0.05,
        user_harm_confirmed=False,  # requires human confirmation; starts False
        output_quality_degraded=signals["output_quality_eval_score"] < 0.6,
        tool_auth_error=signals["tool_call_http_401_rate"] > 0.02,
    )

    pd_service = PD_ROUTING.get(cls.owner_team, "PD_SERVICE_ID_SRE")
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        await client.post(
            "https://events.pagerduty.com/v2/enqueue",
            json={
                "routing_key": pd_service,
                "event_action": "trigger",
                "payload": {
                    "summary": f"[{cls.severity}][{cls.category}] {payload.description}",
                    "severity": cls.severity.lower().replace("sev-", "p"),
                    "custom_details": {
                        "category": cls.category,
                        "rationale": cls.rationale,
                        "page_immediately": cls.page_immediately,
                        "notify_legal": cls.notify_legal,
                        "notify_ciso": cls.notify_ciso,
                    },
                },
            },
        )
    return {"classified_as": cls.category, "severity": cls.severity}
Re-Classification During an Incident

Initial classification happens with incomplete information. As the incident progresses, new evidence changes the picture. The incident commander must re-run the decision tree at each new information milestone.
T+5 min: user_harm_confirmed flips True → Safety SEV-3 becomes SEV-1 → page Legal, notify executive on-call.
T+10 min: security team identifies prompt injection in trace → category upgrades from Safety to Security → CISO notification triggered.
T+15 min: infrastructure restores, error rate drops → re-classification confirms Capability, severity downgrades to SEV-3 → SRE stands down.

Severity can only increase during an incident, never decrease during active triage. An incident that starts at SEV-3 and gathers evidence of user harm is upgraded to SEV-2 or SEV-1. Downgrade happens only after mitigation is confirmed and metrics have returned to normal for at least 30 minutes.
Runbook Pointer by Classification Output

# runbook-index.yaml
# Loaded by the incident CLI to surface the correct runbook from a Classification.
runbooks:
  capability:
    SEV-1:
      url: runbooks/capability/sev1-capability-outage.md
      summary: "Complete capability loss — switch to fallback model, halt affected endpoints"
    SEV-2:
      url: runbooks/capability/sev2-quality-degradation.md
      summary: "Quality degradation with user impact — rate limit sessions, enable fallback"
    SEV-3:
      url: runbooks/capability/sev3-eval-alert.md
      summary: "Proactive eval alert — investigate root cause, monitor for escalation"

  infrastructure:
    SEV-1:
      url: runbooks/infra/sev1-outage.md
      summary: "Full infrastructure outage — activate DR site, page SRE leadership"
    SEV-2:
      url: runbooks/infra/sev2-degraded.md
      summary: "Partial infra failure — scale out, switch to backup endpoint"
    SEV-3:
      url: runbooks/infra/sev3-alert.md
      summary: "Non-critical infra alert — investigate, no immediate action required"

  safety:
    SEV-1:
      url: runbooks/safety/sev1-harm-confirmed.md
      summary: "Harm confirmed — suspend endpoint, notify Legal, exec on-call"
    SEV-2:
      url: runbooks/safety/sev2-policy-violation.md
      summary: "Policy violation without confirmed harm — page T&S, review session"

  integration:
    SEV-2:
      url: runbooks/integration/sev2-tool-failure.md
      summary: "Tool layer failure with user impact — failover, rotate credentials"

  security:
    SEV-1:
      url: runbooks/security/sev1-breach.md
      summary: "Security breach — kill switch, forensic trace capture, notify CISO"
Key Takeaways

The first question in the tree—proactive vs reactive detection—determines whether safety and security checks are required before severity is assigned. Skipping this question can delay critical notifications by minutes that matter.

Safety and security branches always ask their questions first when user reports arrive. These categories require notifications that take longer to action, so they must be triggered as early as possible.

Encode the tree as code, not as a flowchart PDF. A function that takes observable boolean signals produces a deterministic classification that can be tested, versioned, and integrated into alerting pipelines.

Re-run the decision tree at each new information milestone during the incident. Initial classification is always provisional; updating it as evidence arrives keeps the response team on the correct runbook.
Severity upgrades immediately when new evidence warrants it. Downgrades happen only after confirmed mitigation and a 30-minute stable-metrics window.
